query: Testicular fine needle aspiration cytopathology vs serum FSH for diagnosing azoospermia

retrieved_docs.title: Male Infertility -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Sertoli cell-only syndrome (azoospermia with high FSH levels. Testis biopsy needed for diagnosis.)

retrieved_docs.title: Azoospermia -- Evaluation -- Further Evaluation of Nonobstructive Azoospermia
retrieved_docs.content: Azoospermia is diagnosed with at least 2 separate semen samples for detailed analysis. In NOA, the abnormal hormonal profile suggests a significant spermatogenic abnormality, as evidenced by an elevated FSH level. In cases of a normal hormonal profile, a testicular biopsy can be used to evaluate spermatogenesis further, as it would be the definitive way to diagnose azoospermia from causes like maturational arrest and Sertoli cell-only syndrome. A karyotype analysis, genetic testing, and Y chromosome microdeletion test should be performed to assess genetic abnormalities. Endocrinological abnormalities are diagnosed and then managed accordingly.

retrieved_docs.title: Azoospermia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The role of FSH stimulation in increasing sperm numbers before a testis biopsy and sperm retrieval procedures is controversial and not currently recommended.

retrieved_docs.title: Sertoli Cell–Only Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: FSH levels are routinely checked in infertile men. A study of 125 men with abnormal spermatogenesis and spermatogenic dysfunction suggested that these findings can be reliably predicted (89%) by finding elevated FSH levels over 7.6 IU/L and small testicles with a long axis of no more than 4.6 cm. [25] Higher FSH levels (22.9 IU/L vs. 13.3IU/L) and smaller testicular volumes (13.3 cc vs. 10.4 cc) have sometimes been associated with more severe spermatogenic dysfunction. [26] Favorable readings in this study were associated with an overall 91.7% successful sperm retrieval rate, while unfavorable measurements had only 56% success. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Male Infertility -- Evaluation -- Other Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Normal testosterone and LH with a high FSH are suggestive of primary spermatogenic failure, especially if associated with azoospermia or severe oligozoospermia. (The normal LH indicates proper Leydig cell function, but the high FSH suggests damage to the seminiferous tubules.) Check testicle size and consider karyotyping as well as Y chromosome microdeletion testing. A less severe form with mild oligozoospermia might indicate Sertoli cell dysfunction, causing reduced production of inhibin, which increases FSH.

retrieved_docs.title: Sertoli Cell–Only Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, there is no specific cure or treatment for Sertoli cell-only syndrome or male maturation arrest. However, in some cases, patients with very low sperm counts or even no sperm in the semen can still be considered for assisted reproductive procedures. Microscopic testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) allows for the microsurgical extraction of sperm directly from the patient's testes. It has a much higher success rate of sperm retrieval than testicular sperm aspiration (TESA), which is done with a simple needle stick but does not require a skilled microscopic surgeon. [33] [34] [35] These sperm can be used to fertilize an egg via intracytoplasmic sperm injection (ICSI). [36] Some studies have shown that sperm recovery is better with higher FSH levels in the blood, but this remains controversial. [37] [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Male Infertility -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Seminomatous and non-seminomatous germ cell testicular tumors

retrieved_docs.title: Male Infertility -- Evaluation -- Other Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Testicular biopsy may be indicated in some cases to exclude spermatogenic failure. Testicular biopsy is typically performed in men suspected of ductal obstruction who would generally present as azoospermic with normal hormonal screening tests and normal-sized testes. Vasography may be done at the same time as the biopsy. Sperm and testicular tissue may also be retrieved and frozen for ART when performing the biopsy, but care must be taken to avoid killing the sperm with preservatives. There is a reported discrepancy in findings on testicular biopsy between the 2 sides, so consider bilateral biopsies.

retrieved_docs.title: Azoospermia -- Evaluation -- Other Genetic Disorders
retrieved_docs.content: NOA patients may have normal FSH and LH levels but will often show elevated levels. The size of the testes may be normal, hypotrophic, or atrophic. If the differentiation is still unclear, a testis biopsy may be required.

retrieved_docs.title: Azoospermia -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Testicular Biopsy
retrieved_docs.content: The design of the testicular tissue is heterogeneous, and spermatogenesis occurs in the foci; hence, a biopsy is infrequently utilized as a diagnostic tool. [63] As a rule, testicular characteristics and diagnostic lab findings are good but imperfect indicators of NOA. Hence, testicular sperm extraction (TESE) can be performed in a specialized assisted reproduction center, which permits cryopreservation of sperm and avoids testicular biopsy. A normal testicular biopsy result is suggestive of obstruction, and vasography is required to identify the precise location of the blockage. A formal surgical repair can then be performed or sperm retrieval as appropriate. Depending on the severity of the defect, serum testosterone levels can be low, normal, or high. Only a single testis needs to be biopsied for a histological diagnosis. The larger of the 2 testes should be selected for the procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Male Infertility -- Evaluation -- Summary of Semen Analysis Results with Suggested Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Low testosterone with high FSH and LH suggests possible Klinefelter syndrome. Karyotyping is recommended as Klinefelter syndrome is likely, especially if testes are small and firm bilaterally. If the karyotyping is normal, consider ART, possibly IVF with ICSI. If severe oligozoospermia (<5 million/mL), consider Y chromosomal defects and microdeletions, which typically cause extremely low sperm counts and may occur in up to 20% of infertile men. [108] Such genetic problems can be transmitted to the offspring by ART and IVF with ICSI. Other causes include postcancer chemo and radiation therapy, bilateral testicular trauma or torsions, and significant testicular infections such as mumps. [109] [110] Low testosterone with low or normal FSH and low or normal LH: possible pituitary issues. Check thyroid function, 8 AM cortisol, and a prolactin level to identify hormone problems and prolactinomas.

retrieved_docs.title: Azoospermia -- Evaluation -- Further Evaluation for Obstructive Azoospermia
retrieved_docs.content: In cases of OA, the hormonal profile and testicular volume measurements are generally normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Male Infertility -- Evaluation -- Summary of Semen Analysis Results with Suggested Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Normal testosterone and normal LH with high FSH: suspect abnormal spermatogenesis with seminiferous tubular damage but with normal Leydig cell function. Normal testosterone, normal LH, and normal FSH with normal testes size: evaluate for genital tract obstruction  (especially if poor sperm motility is also present) [111]

retrieved_docs.title: Azoospermia -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: A detailed history, careful physical examination, and initial hormonal screening (testosterone, FSH, LH, prolactin, and estradiol) are necessary to classify the type of azoospermia as obstructive versus nonobstructive. Karyotype analysis and genetic testing for Y chromosome microdeletions should be offered to azoospermic men without an apparent cause of their disorder. Genetic testing is recommended before sperm retrieval procedures or testis biopsies. Selective scrotal ultrasonography and transrectal ultrasound examinations may also be helpful in further diagnosing and classifying azoospermia. Karyotype testing and genetic screening are recommended before sperm retrieval procedures to avoid unnecessary surgeries.

retrieved_docs.title: Azoospermia -- Treatment / Management -- Nonobstructive Azoospermia
retrieved_docs.content: FSH stimulation before GnRH therapy seems to improve the outcomes. GnRH is only effective in men with normal pituitary activity. Gonadotropin treatment with HCG (with or without FSH) is preferred in patients with decreased or absent pituitary function. The suggested dosage is 1000 IU to 3000 IU 2 to 3 times weekly. This typically leads to sperm production after 3 to 6 months. If unsuccessful, FSH is added at a 75 IU to 150 IU dose twice a week. [90] The overall success rate of spermatogenesis from medical therapy of hypogonadotropic hypogonadism is about 75%. If medical treatment is unsuccessful, assisted reproductive techniques are recommended. [88] [91]

retrieved_docs.title: Male Infertility -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Follicle-stimulating hormonal (FSH) abnormalities

retrieved_docs.content: Fine needle biopsy of ovary is a diagnostic procedure performed on female reproductive organs and is classified among other diagnostic procedures for female organs. It is synonymous with the Arabic term “خزعة بإبرة رفيعة,” indicating that this technique is the same method referred to by that phrase.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2188
retrieved_docs.content: Disorder of male reproductive system is a broad category of conditions affecting the male reproductive organs, and within this category سرطان الخصية (testicular cancer) is a specific malignant disorder of the testes, illustrating that testicular cancer is a subset of male reproductive system disorders and highlighting its relevance in male reproductive health and oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2530

Recognizing that other male genital disorders fall under the broader category of prostate disorder and are associated with screening emphasizes the importance of appropriate diagnostic evaluation in the management of these conditions.

These procedures are grouped under “إجراءات هيستوباثولوجيا متعلقة بالأعضاء التناسلية والثدي” and the child category “Other diagnostic procedures; female organs,” which itself is a child of عملية للأعضاء التناسلية الأنثوية (female genital‑organ procedures). خزرعة بطانة الرحم (endometrial biopsy) is the target‑anatomy for both open and diagnostic dilation‑and‑curettage (D&C) of the uterus, illustrating a common diagnostic pathway for uterine pathology. * Aspiration curettage after delivery or abortion, classified as “Dilatation and curettage (D&C); aspiration after delivery or abortion,” also employs الخزعة for tissue retrieval. * Male genital diagnostics, specifically transrectal prostate biopsy, is a child of عمليات البروستاتا (prostate operations) and belongs to “diagnostic procedures; male genital,” highlighting its role in prostate pathology assessment. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2598
retrieved_docs.content: Fine needle biopsy of the ovary is a specific application of the fine needle biopsy technique (خزعة بإبرة رفيعة), utilizing a thin needle to obtain tissue samples from ovarian lesions for diagnostic evaluation. This method allows minimally invasive access to ovarian tissue, facilitating accurate histopathological analysis while reducing procedural morbidity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 539
retrieved_docs.content: Biospecimen Collection Method is defined by the value “الخزعة” (biopsy). The biopsy procedure functions as a method within both diagnostic endocrine procedures and diagnostic procedures; male genital. Within the hierarchy of specimen‑collection techniques, “الخزعة” has a child entity “لطاخة المهبل” (vaginal swab), indicating that vaginal swab is a specific subtype of biopsy‑related collection. Additionally, Transrectal biopsy of prostate is a child of diagnostic procedures; male genital, representing a specialized application of biopsy for prostate evaluation. Together, these relationships illustrate how biopsy serves as a central biospecimen collection method across endocrine and male genital diagnostics, with distinct sub‑procedures such as vaginal swab and transrectal prostate biopsy extending its clinical relevance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1835
retrieved_docs.content: Transrectal biopsy of prostate is classified as a diagnostic procedure within the male genital system, indicating its role in evaluating prostate pathology in men. This procedure is organized under the broader category of male genital diagnostic techniques and is noted to have subordinate or related child procedures that further refine its clinical application.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 699
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on ovaries and diagnostic dilation and curettage of uterus are specific diagnostic interventions that belong to the broader group of other diagnostic procedures; female organs. Both procedures are classified as diagnostic procedures for the female genital system, indicating that they are used to evaluate ovarian and uterine pathology respectively. The general concept “Diagnostic” is intended to encompass these ovarian and uterine diagnostic procedures. Fine needle biopsy of ovary is considered equivalent to the category of other diagnostic procedures; female organs and is performed using the method described in Arabic as “خزعة بإبرة رفيعة” (fine‑needle biopsy). Together, these entities illustrate the hierarchical organization of female‑specific diagnostic techniques, linking individual procedures to their parent category and specifying the method employed for fine‑needle ovarian sampling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: Disorder of the male reproductive system encompasses a range of conditions affecting male reproductive organs, and within this category testicular cancer (سرطان الخصية) represents a specific disease entity. Cancer of the testis is synonymous with testicular cancer and therefore also belongs to the broader group of male reproductive system disorders. This hierarchical relationship clarifies that both the general classification “Disorder of male reproductive system” and the specific term “cancer of testis” are linked under the specific condition identified as سرطـان الخصية.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1639
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage following delivery or abortion is classified as dilatation and curettage (D&C); aspiration after delivery or abortion. The dilatation and curettage (D&C); aspiration after delivery or abortion procedure is placed within the broader category of surgical interventions for female genital organs (عملية للأعضاء التناسلية الأنثوية). This D&C procedure also functions as a defining classification for similar techniques, such as dilatation and curettage (d&c); aspiration after delivery or abortion. Within the same parent category of female genital organ procedures, uterine hysteroscopy (منظار الرحم) is identified as a related child procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2808
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration curettage following delivery or abortion is classified as a form of dilatation and curettage (D&C) performed as an aspiration procedure after delivery or abortion.
